pages.raw_text:
Daniel J. Keefe, who was APP
pointed commissioner general of
immigration to succeed the late
Frank p. Sargent by President
Roosevelt last week, Is on interest
ing personality in many respects.
Back 20 years ago he was dock
walloper" on the river front in
Cleveland and Chicago. In those
days, before disciplinary and educa-
tional methods were introduced by
the union, the lockers were a
rough and ready lot of men, who
would as soon fight as eat. 1t was
before the advent of machinery,
when the loading and unloading of
iron ore, grain, lumber and other
freight was p'.-ely matter of mus
cular ability, and when the man
who could carry the heaviest loads
and work the longest hours was re-
garded as the king of the docks. y
Keefe was a pretty husky fellow
himself and could hold his own
with the strong men and he was
also credited with having "a good
head" withal. He was one of the
most active spirits In organizing
the longshoremen, and when he as
sumed the presidency of the asso-
citation, dozen years ago. he
mapped out campaign that re-
suited in the amalgamation of
many separate national and local
bodies. So aggressive did Keefe
become about 1900 in claiming jur-
isdiction over nearly all classes of
work afloat and along shore that
he aroused the ire of seamen, rail-
road freight handlers and team
sters, who disputed his "trustifica-
ton" schemes.


A great advantage was gained by
Keefe when the late Senator M. A.
Hanna extended his sympathetic
support to establish annual agree
ments between the employers asso-
Citations in the marine industry and
the ongshoremen's union, which
adopted the more dignified title of
Transport Wokers' association"
to the great disgust of the seamen,
who were not, only fighting the
longshoremen. but were unable to
obtain pleasant look from the
vessel owners. The joint agree
ment plan promoted by Senator
Hanna was source of pride to
him, and the relations between the
organized employers and workers
was continued up to this year,
when they switched about and de-
clared for the "open shop"


One of Keefe's prominent charac-
teristics is his pronounced tacitur-
nifty. A. the recent Denver conven-
ton of the American Federation of
Labor the followers of President
Gompers resorted to every method
to make Keefe's position unpleas-
ant and goad him into fight, and
one committee officially condemned
him for his support of Taft. But
Keefe remained exasperatingly i'm
perturable and refused to be drawn
into debate.


It was doubtless due to his re-
served and sscretive nature that
Keefe was selected to perform q
delicate task by Senator Hanna in
the early part OF the campaign OF
1900. The anthracite miners were
on strike. and Hanna, recalling that
the Homestead strike of 1892 was
of sufficient Importance to turn the
balance In favor of Grover Cleve.
land and defeat Harrison. was par
ticularly anxious that McKiniey's
chance OF reelection should not be
endangered by any industrial dis
turbance. TIE newspapers con
